perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without reference to the conflicts of laws principles thereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of England, without reference to the conflicts of laws principles thereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to England removes certain consumer protections afforded under Delaware corporate law, specifically regarding minority shareholder rights in dispute resolution, potentially violating Delaware law if disputes arise concerning the Joint Venture's actions within Delaware.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law, specifically sections dealing with shareholder rights and dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of Delaware law grants shareholders the right to inspect corporate books and records under certain conditions, a right that might be curtailed or altered under English law, thus contradicting Delaware's statutory provisions designed to protect shareholder interests.

General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meeting substantially concurrently with such proceedings, and (iii) if any stockholder or proxyholder vo
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Delaware law, which includes consumer protections and shareholder rights. By changing the governing law to England, the modified text eliminates the legally mandated consumer protections afforded under Delaware corporate law, specifically regarding minority shareholder rights in dispute resolution. The provided snippet references Delaware law (Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1), indicating that the contract initially intended to adhere to Delaware's corporate regulations. The absence of these protections under English law creates a legal contradiction, as it removes a legally mandated consumer protection that was initially in place, making the contract non-compliant as described under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to indemnify and hold the other harmless against any and all liability to any broker retained by such Party in connection with this Agreement and the transactions contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to resolve any liability to any broker retained by such Party in connection with this Agreement and the transactions contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the indemnity clause with a 'commercially reasonable efforts' clause weakens the protection against broker liabilities. Under Delaware law, specifically related to fiduciary duties, officers can be strictly liable for breaches involving retained brokers who act counter to the business's interest. This weakening violates that protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law concerning fiduciary duty of care and loyalty, especially in the context of broker relationships.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 141 outlines the powers and duties of directors. By weakening the indemnification, the contract makes it harder to enforce the directors' duty of care concerning broker selection and oversight, potentially shielding them from liabilities they would otherwise face under Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The change from an indemnity clause to a 'commercially reasonable efforts' clause weakens the protection against broker liabilities. The original text provides a stronger guarantee of protection than the modified version. The Perturbation Explanation states that this weakening potentially violates Delaware law related to fiduciary duties. The Law Explanation suggests this shift makes it harder to enforce directors' duty of care. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly subsection (a), states that the business and affairs of every corporation shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors. The modified text, by weakening indemnification, makes it more difficult for the board to uphold its management duties, creating a contradiction with the law, as the 'commercially reasonable efforts' may not suffice to fully address liabilities, especially given the strict liability standards mentioned in the Perturbation Explanation. The omission of the indemnity clause creates a loophole, fitting the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: From time to time, each Party shall have the right to have its own internal or external auditors review the books and records of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: From time to time, each Party may request access to summarized financial reports of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the audit right to only 'summarized financial reports' limits access to full books and records. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, which mandates transparency in joint ventures. Full access is usually considered a required shareholder protection in close corporations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contract law.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware case law establishing the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing (see *Nemec v. Shrader*, 991 A.2d 1120 (Del. 2010))
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware case law, like *Nemec v. Shrader*, clarifies the implied covenant requires parties to act reasonably and honestly. Limiting audit access to 'summarized reports' could be seen as hindering a party's ability to assess the venture's performance, potentially violating the good faith obligation to provide adequate information.

perturbation.justification: The original text grants the right to 'review the books and records,' whereas the changed text limits this right to 'summarized financial reports.' This constitutes an omission of essential information related to auditing rights. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this undermines transparency and shareholder protection under Delaware law, aligning with the definition of an 'Omission Legal Contradiction.' The scraped law snippet 1 mentions 'Title 8 - Corporations' within the Delaware Code, suggesting that corporate governance and shareholder rights, including access to financial information, are legally regulated in Delaware. By restricting access, the changed text contradicts the expected duty of good faith and fair dealing by limiting transparency.
